content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901          322-6441              322-6444

August 25, 1981


Lawrence L. Duga, Esq.
Attorney at Law
1440 Broadway, Suite 1000
Oakland, CA  94612

Dear Mr. Duga:

     This letter is sent in response to your letter request-
ing information as to the powers and duties of filing
officers under the Political Reform Act.  Specifically you
questioned the extent of Mr. Jonathan Redding's authority
over the filing of campaign statements.  My understanding
is that Mr. Redding is an employee in the Berkeley City
Clerk's office.  As I explained to you in our telephone
conversation of August 18, 1981, I cannot advise you as to
the full extent of Mr. Redding's authority or duties;
however, I can delineate the role and authority of a filing
officer as to statements filed pursuant to the Political
Reform Act.

     Government Code Section 82027 1/ defines a "filing
officer" as "the office or officer with whom any statement
or report is required to be filed under this title."  The
Act further specifies in Section 81010 that the duties of a
filing officer are to:

     (a)  Supply the necessary forms and manuals prescribed
by the Commission;

     (b)  Determine whether required documents have been
filed and, if so, whether they conform on their face
with the requirements of this title;

     (c)  Notify promptly all persons and known committees
who have failed to file a report or statement in the form
and at the time required by this title;

     (d)  Report apparent violations of this title to the
appropriate agencies;


___________________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code.
     Code.

Lawrence L. Duga, Esq.
August 25, 1981
Page Two


     (e)  Compile and maintain a current list of all reports
and statements filed with this office.

     As you can see, the Act requires a filing officer to
ensure that statements conform on their face with the Act
and to report any violations of the Act which come to his
or her attention to the appropriate enforcement
authorities.2/

     In addition, filing officers are required to assess
fees for the late filing of statements and reports.  Sec-
tion 91013.  The authority to charge a late fee may be
exercised only when a statement is actually filed late and
not to cure defects in a statement filed by the deadline.
The Commission determined in the Opinion requested by
Rex E. Layton, 1 FPPC Opinions 113 (No. 75-025, August 21,
1975) (copy enclosed), that late filing fees should not be
assessed when a campaign statement is in substantial com-
pliance with the statute.  Any late fees should be charged
against the person or persons responsible under the Act for
the filing of the statements.  The filing officer has no
authority to impose penalties other than late filing fees
for violations of the Act.  No formal process of appeal
exists from the acts of a filing officer in assessing late
fees.

     As to Mr. Redding's power to enforce the Berkeley ordi-
nance or exact penalties for its violation, we cannot
advise except to say that the Act makes no provision for
the enforcement of local campaign ordinances.

     I trust you will find this discussion responsive to
your questions.  If I can be of further assistance, please
feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMF:plh
Enclosure
cc:  Jonathan W. Redding


_________________________

     2/  Section 91001 spells out which agencies bear the
responsibility for enforcement of the Act.

LAWRENCE L. DUGA
ATTORNEY AT LAW
1440 BROADWAY
SUITE 1000
OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612
TELEPHONE (415) 452-1300

July 14, 1981

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA. 95804

Gentlepersons:

I seek an informal opinion or information regarding the
powers of one Jonathan W. Redding. Jonathan W. Redding
is an employee of the City of Berkeley in the City Clerk's
Office. He has been assigned as staff to the Fair Campaign
Practices Commission of the City of Berkeley. He describes
himself as a "compliance officer" under the state law and
claims the right to make determinations separate and apart
from any determination made by the Berkeley Fair Campaign
Practices Commission. Specifically, I would like to know
the following:

1. Is there such a position as "compliance officer" and does
Mr. Redding hold it?

2. The City Clerk is designated the filing officer under
the state law. Can that position be "transferred" by the
City Clerk to someone else, i.e. to an employee of the City
working in the City Clerk's Department?

3. Does the filing officer have the power to levy fines;

a. Under state law for "on the face" or apparent
violation of the state law;

b. Under state law for "on the face" or apparent
violations of the Berkeley Ordinance covering campaign disclos-
ures;

c. Under state law for determinations made by the
filing officer that the state law has been violated (e.g. that a
non-monetary contribution has been listed at less than its
fair market value); and

d. Under state law for determinations made by the
filing officer that the Berkeley Ordinance has been violated.

-1-

Letter to Pair Political Practices Commission
July 14, 1981
Page Two

4. If the filing officer has the power to levy fines,against
whom may they be levied? Treasurers, candidates, others?

5. If the filing officer has the power to levy fines, what process of appeal exists?

Very truly yours,

[signature]

LAWRENCE L. DUGA

cc: Lee Halterman

Advice Letter to Lawrence Duga, Oakland, August 25, 1981

A filing officer has limited authority with respect to viola-
tions of the Political Reform Act by persons who file campaign
statements. The filing officer may assess late fees for statements
which are not filed on time. Although the filing officer cannot
impose penalties for any other violations of the Act, he or she may
refer statements which appear to violate the Act to the appropri-
ate enforcement authorities. Late filing fees may not be imposed
for statements which are timely filed, but which contain defects or
apparent violations of the Act. See 1 FPPC Opinions 113 (Opinion
requested by Rex E. Layton, 1975).

sections.conclusion: A filing officer has limited authority with respect to violations of the Political Reform Act by persons who file campaign statements. The filing officer may assess late fees for statements which are not filed on time. Although the filing officer cannot impose penalties for any other violations of the Act, he or she may refer statements which appear to violate the Act to the appropriate enforcement authorities. Late filing fees may not be imposed for statements which are timely filed, but which contain defects or apparent violations of the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: What are the powers and duties of a filing officer under the Political Reform Act, specifically regarding Jonathan Redding's authority as a compliance officer in the Berkeley City Clerk's office to assess fines and penalties for violations of campaign disclosure requirements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Filing officers have limited authority: they may only assess late filing fees for statements filed past the deadline, cannot impose other penalties for violations, and must refer apparent violations to appropriate enforcement authorities. Late fees cannot be charged for timely-filed statements that contain defects or violations.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both incoming request letter (July 14, 1981) and FPPC response letter (August 25, 1981). Extracted from the FPPC advice letter portion. Summary at bottom of document provided verbatim conclusion. Minor OCR quality issues present but did not impede extraction.

Government Code Section 820271:/ defines a "filing

tion 91013.

Any late fees should be charged 
against the person or persons responsible under the Act for

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to attorney Duga's inquiry regarding the powers of Jonathan Redding, a compliance officer in Berkeley's City Clerk office, clarifying that filing officers under the Political Reform Act may only assess late filing fees and must refer violations to enforcement authorities, with no authority to impose other penalties.
